title: COVID-19 Vaccine Development — how pharma did the impossible
excerpt: Vaccines approved in under a year. The fastest before this had taken four years. Here is how it actually happened — the science, the money, the regulatory shortcuts that were not shortcuts, and why India sat at the centre of it.
llmsTopic: COVID-19 vaccines, mRNA vaccines, Covishield, Covaxin, Serum Institute, Bharat Biotech, rolling review, vaccine development India
llmsSummary: Explains why vaccines normally take 10 to 15 years and what structurally changed during COVID through parallel development funded by government advance market commitments. Covers the three platform technologies, how rolling regulatory review worked, India's specific role with Serum Institute producing over 200 million doses per month, and what permanently changed in platform science and supply chains.

title: Lipitor's Patent Cliff — how the world's best-selling drug lost everything overnight
excerpt: Lipitor generated over $125 billion before its patent expired in 2011. The full story of Pfizer's pre-cliff strategy, the Ranbaxy legal fight, and what the playbook teaches about pharma lifecycle management.
llmsTopic: Lipitor, atorvastatin, Pfizer, patent cliff, generic drugs, lifecycle management, pay-for-delay, Ranbaxy
llmsSummary: Covers how Lipitor became the world's best-selling drug and Pfizer's pre-cliff strategies including a 17.5 percent price increase in the final year and one billion dollars annually in direct-to-consumer advertising. Explains the pay-for-delay settlement with Ranbaxy in 2008 and the three defensive strategies Pfizer executed on the day the patent expired in November 2011.

title: The Bayer–Monsanto Merger — how a $63 billion agricultural bet destroyed a pharmaceutical legacy
excerpt: Bayer went to bed a pharmaceutical company and woke up an agrochemical defendant. The $63 billion Monsanto acquisition starved the pharma pipeline, triggered $17 billion in litigation, and wiped out €35 billion in market value.
llmsTopic: Bayer, Monsanto, merger, glyphosate, Roundup, pharma pipeline, capital allocation, shareholder revolt
llmsSummary: Covers Bayer's pre-deal pipeline problem and Werner Baumann's unsolicited 63 billion dollar all-cash bid that bypassed a shareholder vote through debt financing. Explains how the glyphosate litigation produced over 17 billion dollars in provisions, how agricultural liabilities starved the pharmaceutical R&D pipeline, and the historic 55 percent shareholder vote against management in 2019.

title: The Thalidomide Tragedy — and the untold Indian chapter
excerpt: A sedative sold as the safest drug in the world caused birth defects in over 10,000 children. Here is what happened, why India's story is more complicated than most people know, and what it permanently changed about how drugs get approved.
llmsTopic: thalidomide, drug safety history, Frances Kelsey, FDA, Kefauver-Harris Amendments, teratogenicity, Indian pharma history, Goa
llmsSummary: Full timeline of the thalidomide crisis including Frances Kelsey's FDA refusal and the specific fetal vulnerability window between gestational days 20 and 36. Covers India's complicated story where Goa as a Portuguese colony received thalidomide shipments in 1959 while mainland India blocked it. Explains what the Kefauver-Harris Amendments of 1962 permanently changed about drug approval worldwide.

title: Ranbaxy and the FDA — How India's biggest pharma success story collapsed
excerpt: India's biggest pharma success story collapsed under data fraud allegations — and permanently changed how generic drug manufacturers are scrutinised worldwide.
llmsTopic: Ranbaxy, FDA, data fraud, generic drugs, GMP, bioequivalence, whistleblower, Dinesh Thakur, GDUFA
llmsSummary: Covers Ranbaxy's rise from Amritsar distributor to the world's third-largest generic manufacturer and the whistleblower investigation by Dinesh Thakur that uncovered systematic fabrication of bioequivalence and stability data. Explains the FDA's Application Integrity Policy, the 500 million dollar criminal settlement in 2013, and how the case permanently changed generic drug auditing globally.

title: The Vioxx Withdrawal — blockbuster economics, selective science, and the fall of a $20 billion drug
excerpt: Merck pulled its blockbuster painkiller in 2004 after a placebo-controlled trial proved an undeniable link to heart attacks. The $7 billion fallout permanently changed how drug safety is monitored worldwide.
llmsTopic: Vioxx, rofecoxib, Merck, COX-2 inhibitor, drug safety, pharmacovigilance, FDA Sentinel Initiative, clinical trial registration
llmsSummary: Explains what COX-2 inhibitors were solving and the VIGOR trial's four-fold cardiovascular risk signal that Merck managed through the Naproxen Hypothesis. Covers how the commercial machinery used training scripts for reps and ghostwritten journals to suppress the signal, why the APPROVe placebo-controlled trial made denial impossible, and what permanently changed including the FDA Sentinel Initiative and mandatory clinical trial registration.
